title: Introduction to Computer Science
description: A first course in computational thinking and programming using Python. Covers variables, loops, functions, and basic data structures. No prior experience required. Open to all majors.

title: Data Structures and Algorithms
description: Core data structures (lists, trees, graphs, hash tables) and the algorithms that operate on them. Emphasis on analyzing time and space complexity. Prerequisite: CSCI-C 101.

title: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
description: Foundations of AI including search, knowledge representation, machine learning, and neural networks. Hands-on projects building intelligent agents.

title: Ethics and the Information Age
description: Moral questions raised by computing and data: privacy, algorithmic bias, automation, and the ethics of artificial intelligence. Designed for students from any background; no technical prerequisites.

title: Introduction to Ethics
description: Major ethical theories from Aristotle to the present, applied to contemporary dilemmas in medicine, business, and public life. A gateway course for non-majors.

title: Foundations of Entrepreneurship
description: How new ventures are created and launched. Students develop a business idea, validate it with customers, and pitch to a panel. Covers lean startup methods and basic financing.

title: Introduction to Financial Accounting
description: Reading and preparing financial statements, the accounting cycle, and how businesses report performance to investors and regulators.

title: Digital Marketing and Analytics
description: Strategies for marketing in digital channels: search, social, email, and content. Students measure campaigns with web analytics and present data-driven recommendations.

title: Introductory Psychology
description: A survey of human behavior and mental processes: perception, memory, development, personality, and mental health. The foundation course for the psychology major.

title: Behavioral Neuroscience
description: How the brain produces behavior. Topics include neurons, brain anatomy, sensation, emotion, learning, and the biology of psychological disorders.

title: Methods of Experimental Psychology
description: Designing experiments, collecting and analyzing behavioral data, and writing scientific reports. Includes a gentle introduction to statistics for the social sciences.

title: Applied Statistical Methods
description: Practical statistics for researchers and analysts: summarizing data, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, and regression. Uses real datasets and statistical software rather than heavy mathematical proofs.

title: Foundations of Data Science
description: An applied, code-light introduction to working with data: cleaning, visualizing, and drawing conclusions. Aimed at students from any discipline who want to make sense of data.

title: Machine Learning in Practice
description: Building and evaluating predictive models with modern Python libraries. Covers regression, classification, clustering, and responsible deployment. Prerequisite: DSCI-D 200 or STAT-S 301.

title: Creative Writing: Fiction and Poetry
description: A workshop for new writers. Read widely and produce original short stories and poems, giving and receiving constructive feedback in a supportive studio setting.

title: Introduction to Literary Analysis
description: Close reading of novels, plays, and poems, with attention to how literature reflects culture and history. Builds the writing and argument skills used across the humanities.

title: American History Since 1865
description: The United States from Reconstruction to the present: industrialization, civil rights, war, and the rise of modern media. Emphasis on primary sources and historical argument.

title: Technology and Society in World History
description: How inventions from the printing press to the internet reshaped human societies. Explores the social consequences of technological change across centuries and cultures.

title: Molecular Biology and Genetics
description: DNA, genes, and how cells use genetic information. Includes laboratory work in modern molecular techniques. A core course for biology and pre-health students.

title: Ecology and Conservation
description: How organisms interact with their environment and each other, and what that means for protecting biodiversity in a changing climate. Field trips included.

title: Urban Sustainability and Climate Policy
description: How cities respond to climate change through policy, planning, and community action. Covers energy, transportation, and equity in environmental decision-making.

title: Introduction to Public Health
description: The science and policy of keeping populations healthy: epidemiology, prevention, health systems, and the social factors that shape well-being.

title: History of Rock and Popular Music
description: From blues and jazz roots to hip-hop and electronic music. Listen critically and understand how popular music reflects social movements. No musical background required.

title: Fundamentals of Music Theory
description: Notation, scales, chords, and rhythm. The building blocks for reading and writing music. Designed for beginners and self-taught musicians.

title: Using Technology in Teaching
description: How educators use digital tools to design engaging lessons and assess learning. Hands-on practice with classroom technology and an introduction to AI tutoring tools.

title: Educational Psychology
description: How students learn and stay motivated. Applies psychological research to teaching practice, classroom management, and supporting diverse learners.

title: Introduction to Microeconomics
description: How individuals and firms make decisions and how markets allocate resources. Covers supply and demand, competition, and the role of government.

title: Data and Economic Decisions
description: Using data to answer economic and policy questions. Students learn to read evidence critically and run simple analyses, with minimal prior math required.

title: Digital Storytelling and Media
description: Reporting and storytelling across text, audio, video, and social platforms. Students produce a multimedia portfolio and learn the ethics of digital journalism.

title: Human-Computer Interaction and Design
description: Designing technology that people actually want to use. Covers user research, prototyping, usability testing, and accessibility. Project-based and open to non-programmers.

title: Social Informatics
description: How information technology shapes society and vice versa: social media, surveillance, the digital divide, and the future of work. Reading and discussion intensive.

title: Calculus I
description: Limits, derivatives, and integrals with applications in science and engineering. The first course in the calculus sequence.

title: Mathematics for Everyday Life
description: Practical math for citizens and consumers: interpreting statistics in the news, personal finance, probability, and spotting misleading numbers. For students nervous about math.

title: Foundations of Nursing Practice
description: Core clinical skills and the science of patient care. Combines lab simulations with the ethical and communication foundations of the nursing profession.

title: Introduction to American Politics
description: How American government works: the Constitution, elections, Congress, the courts, and the role of citizens. Connects current events to enduring debates.

title: Data Mining (Graduate)
description: Advanced techniques for discovering patterns in large datasets: clustering, association rules, dimensionality reduction, and scalable algorithms. For graduate students with a programming and statistics background.
